items.title: Trump signs executive order on AI innovation and security
items.summary: President Trump signed an executive order on June 2 establishing a framework for secure deployment of frontier AI models and federal cybersecurity efforts.
items.details: The order directs federal agencies to set up a voluntary framework allowing developers to give the government early access to frontier AI models for up to 30 days before release. It also expands use of advanced AI tools for cybersecurity while favoring a light-touch regulatory approach.

items.title: Executive order reclassifies about 8,000 federal positions
items.summary: President Trump signed an executive order on June 3 reclassifying roughly 8,000 senior policy-influencing federal positions into a new Schedule Policy/Career category.
items.details: The order designates the positions as at-will roles, allowing agencies to remove employees for poor performance, misconduct, or subversion of directives without certain procedural steps. The White House said the positions remain career roles with non-partisan hiring processes and removals made without regard to political affiliation.

items.title: President signs Secure America Act into law
items.summary: On June 10, the President signed S. 2, the Secure America Act, providing immigration enforcement funding through Fiscal Year 2029.
items.details: The law appropriates funding to the Department of Homeland Security, Customs and Border Protection, and Immigration and Customs Enforcement for immigration enforcement and related activities through FY2029. It was enacted as Public Law 119-98.

items.title: US and Iran announce initial deal to extend ceasefire
items.summary: The United States and Iran announced an agreement extending their ceasefire for 60 days, with a memorandum of understanding set to be signed June 19.
items.details: The framework is intended to reopen the Strait of Hormuz to commercial shipping and create a window for further negotiations. The fate of Iran's nuclear program remains unresolved and is set to be addressed in upcoming talks.

items.title: US plans to reduce military assets for NATO operations in Europe
items.summary: The United States plans to significantly reduce the aircraft and warships it makes available for NATO operations in Europe, according to two senior European officials.
items.details: The plan was communicated to allies in early June in a written document. The Pentagon declined to comment on specific numbers and referred to a prior statement by its European Command about intentions to reduce commitments in Europe.

items.title: House passes U.S.-Japan-ROK Trilateral Cooperation Act
items.summary: The U.S. House of Representatives passed the bipartisan U.S.-Japan-ROK Trilateral Cooperation Act on June 8.
items.details: The legislation, introduced by Rep. Ami Bera, addresses national security and foreign policy cooperation among the United States, Japan, and South Korea. The House also passed the bipartisan DOMINANCE Act on critical minerals the same day.

items.title: Senate passes Secure America Act
items.summary: The U.S. Senate passed the Secure America Act on June 5, providing roughly $70 billion for immigration enforcement and border security through 2029.
items.details: The Senate-passed bill provides $38.5 billion for ICE and $22.6 billion for CBP, plus additional border security and DHS funding. The measure was advanced through budget reconciliation and required a simple majority.

items.title: House passes Ukraine Support Act
items.summary: The U.S. House passed H.R. 2913, the Ukraine Support Act, on June 4.
items.details: The bill passed on a simple-majority vote on passage in the House. Records of the vote are maintained by the House Clerk and posted at house.gov.

items.title: FISA Section 702 authorization lapses amid funding votes
items.summary: During the week of June 8, the House passed several anti-fraud bills and extended DHS funding through 2029, while FISA's Section 702 authorization lapsed.
items.details: According to a legislative recap, many bills passed by voice vote with a few roll call votes. The Section 702 surveillance authorization lapsed, though the underlying program was reported likely to continue uninterrupted.

items.title: Supreme Court permits Alabama to use disputed congressional map
items.summary: The Supreme Court cleared the way for Alabama to use a congressional map in the 2026 elections that lower courts found to be racially discriminatory.
items.details: The court issued an unsigned order allowing the map's use after sending the case back to a lower court in light of its Louisiana v. Callais decision. Justice Sotomayor dissented, joined by Justices Kagan and Jackson.

items.title: Federal judge strikes down immigration proceeding freeze
items.summary: A federal judge on June 5 struck down a Trump administration policy that made it harder for immigrants from dozens of countries to stay in or enter the United States.
items.details: U.S. District Chief Judge John McConnell Jr. ruled the policy left many immigrants in legal limbo and said U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services ignored the law. The policy applied to USCIS and did not affect immigration judges granting asylum at the border.

items.title: Federal judge strikes down $100,000 H-1B visa fee
items.summary: A federal judge in Boston struck down the Trump administration's $100,000 fee on new H-1B visas.
items.details: U.S. District Judge Leo Sorokin sided with 20 states, concluding the executive branch exceeded its authority and violated the Administrative Procedure Act. Related lawsuits are pending in other federal courts, raising the possibility of divided appellate rulings.

items.title: Supreme Court declines to block California's redrawn map
items.summary: The Supreme Court declined to take up an emergency appeal from California Republicans, clearing the state's newly redrawn congressional map for the 2026 midterms.
items.details: The map shifts five seats in Democrats' favor and follows voter approval of Proposition 50. State Republicans, joined by the Department of Justice, had sued; the justices' decision included no dissents or explanation.

items.title: Mid-decade redistricting continues across multiple states
items.summary: Several states have pursued mid-decade congressional redistricting ahead of the 2026 midterms, with some maps enacted and others tied up in litigation.
items.details: States including California, Missouri, North Carolina, Ohio, Texas, and Utah have new congressional maps, while South Carolina and others have considered redrawing. Many of the new maps face ongoing court challenges over their use in 2026.

items.title: Georgia holds primary runoff elections
items.summary: Georgia's primary runoff elections are scheduled for June 16 in races where no candidate reached the 50% threshold in the May 19 primary.
items.details: Runoffs apply to partisan primaries for U.S. Senate, U.S. House, and other offices, as well as certain nonpartisan judicial and consolidated city-county government races. The general election is set for November 3, with runoffs December 1.

CA.title: Newsom signs election interference law ahead of primary
CA.summary: Gov. Gavin Newsom signed Senate Bill 73 on May 27, strengthening California's election protections ahead of the June 2 statewide primary.
CA.details: The law criminalizes taking cast ballots from local election officials' custody, prohibits warrantless searches of voting machines and voter rolls, and allows state officials to seek injunctive relief and civil penalties. It took effect immediately under an urgency clause.

FL.title: Legislature passes property tax amendment for November ballot
FL.summary: The Florida Legislature passed HJR 1F on June 2 during a special session, sending a property tax constitutional amendment to voters on November 3.
FL.details: The amendment would increase the homestead exemption for non-school property taxes, reaching $250,000 by 2028. It requires 60% voter approval to be ratified. Gov. DeSantis said he may seek a fall special session to restore provisions the Legislature removed.

SC.title: 2026 legislative session begins with all seats filled
SC.summary: South Carolina's 2026 legislative session began June 13 with all 170 seats filled after a special election in Dorchester County.
SC.details: Republican Greg Ford maintained a 21-vote lead in an automatic recount to win the final vacant state House seat. Earlier, Gov. McMaster called a special session on redistricting and the budget; the Senate had not advanced a new congressional map.

TX.title: Redrawn congressional map remains in effect for 2026 primaries
TX.summary: Texas's redrawn congressional map (PlanC2333) is in effect for the 2026 elections after a U.S. Supreme Court stay, pending appeal.
TX.details: A federal district court enjoined the map in November, but the Supreme Court granted Texas a stay on December 4. The map remains in use for congressional elections beginning with the 2026 primaries pending the court's action on the appeal.

VA.title: State Supreme Court nullifies redistricting referendum
VA.summary: The Supreme Court of Virginia nullified the 2026 redistricting referendum and new map on May 8 after voters had approved the measure.
VA.details: The legislatively referred constitutional amendment, which would have allowed mid-decade congressional redistricting, passed by a slim margin on the April 21 ballot. The state supreme court subsequently struck it down.

MD.title: Federal workforce reclassification draws state attention
MD.summary: Maryland outlets reported on the federal executive order moving about 8,000 federal jobs into a category that strips certain job protections, affecting many Maryland residents.
MD.details: The June 3 order revives a version of the previously proposed Schedule F policy, renamed Schedule Policy/Career. Maryland, home to many federal workers, saw coverage of affected employees facing decisions under the change.

disclaimer: This digest reports verifiable developments from cited sources without endorsement of any party, candidate, or position.
